user_message_example: I cannot stand people who go after what they want. They seem shallow and disgusting.
user_patterns: I moralize what I cannot do
I feel bitter when others act
I judge what I secretly envy
nietzschean_angle: Ressentiment is not mere anger. It is bitterness that becomes a value system. What one fears, envies, or cannot reach gets reclassified as morally bad.
plain_explanation: Sometimes the judgment is real. But sometimes moral condemnation is wounded pride in disguise. The person is not only saying 'that is wrong'; they are also defending themselves against humiliation, envy, or impotence.
sharp_reply_style: Probe whether the judgment is reactive. Do not shame the user. Expose the possibility that hatred is a defense against failed desire or injured pride.
primary_references: On the Genealogy of Morality - Ressentiment and the Slave Revolt
card_text:
Theme: ressentiment
User example: I cannot stand people who go after what they want. They seem shallow and disgusting.
User patterns: I hate ambitious people, successful people disgust me, I moralize what I cannot do, I feel bitter when others act, I judge what I secretly envy
Nietzschean angle: Ressentiment is not mere anger. It is bitterness that becomes a value system. What one fears, envies, or cannot reach gets reclassified as morally bad.
Plain explanation: Sometimes the judgment is real. But sometimes moral condemnation is wounded pride in disguise. The person is not only saying 'that is wrong'; they are also defending themselves against humiliation, envy, or impotence.
Sharp reply style: Probe whether the judgment is reactive. Do not shame the user. Expose the possibility that hatred is a defense against failed desire or injured pride.
Primary references: On the Genealogy of Morality - Ressentiment and the Slave Revolt, Guide - Ressentiment Explained, Guide - Reactive Morality Explained
Source IDs: genealogy_ressentiment_excerpt, guide_genealogy_ressentiment_explained, guide_genealogy_reactive_morality_explained
Tags: ressentiment, envy, bitterness, moralization, reactivity
